agent1_snippets.title: Osteomalacia -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Objectives: Identify the underlying causes of osteomalacia, including vitamin D, calcium, and phosphate deficiencies. Implement appropriate testing, such as serum vitamin D levels, bone mineral density scans, and parathyroid hormone measurements, to help confirm the osteomalacia diagnosis. Apply evidence-based guidelines to select the most suitable treatment modalities, including vitamin D supplementation, dietary adjustments, and underlying disease management. Collaborate with an interprofessional team to coordinate and enhance the delivery of care for patients with osteomalacia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

agent1_snippets.title: Dual-Energy X-Ray Absorptiometry -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Evaluation of primary and secondary osteoporosis cannot be elucidated with DEXA imaging. This limitation was exemplified in a study showing that 55 out of 173 women (32%) diagnosed with primary osteoporosis were found to have secondary causes, most often calcium disorders and hyperparathyroidism. The study suggested that additional testing for all women, including 24-hour urine calcium, serum calcium, serum parathyroid hormone, and serum thyroid-stimulating hormone for women on thyroid replacement therapy, is cost-effective in identifying secondary causes of osteoporosis. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Complications
agent1_snippets.content: Hyperphosphatemia complexes serum calcium, decreasing the levels of ionized calcium, and triggering the release of PTH, resulting in secondary hyperparathyroidism; this causes high bone turnover state, releasing calcium from the bone, to normalize the serum calcium level.

agent1_snippets.title: Normocalcemic Hyperparathyroidism  -- Evaluation -- Additional imaging studies
agent1_snippets.content: DEXA  (dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry) scans of the lumbar spine, the hip, and the distal third of the radius determine the T score based on the standard deviation compared to a young healthy adult's bone density. [58] T scores are always negative, reflecting some loss of bone mineral density over time compared to the theoretical optimal level. [58] Although a good measure of bone density, the T score does not indicate bone strength or quality. Fracture sites, arthritis, and degenerative bony changes all influence values and must be considered when interpreting the DEXA data. [58] A T score of -2.5 or lower is generally considered diagnostic of osteoporosis. [58] A trabecular bone score is the most cost-effective way of determining bone quality and is an indirect analysis of trabecular microstructure extracted from data obtained from the DEXA. [59] [60] The trabecular bone score demonstrates decreased connectivity of the trabecular spinal microstructure in patients with PHPT, although this may appear normal on the DEXA scan. [60] [61]

agent1_snippets.title: Hungry Bone Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Unfortunately, no specific level of PTH has been identified where the risk for HBS is proportionate enough to suggest the development of a clinical calculator or indicator. However, in primary hyperparathyroidism, PTH levels are more often subtly elevated, ranging from 300 to 400 pg/mL, compared to the higher range of 700 to 1000 pg/mL seen in patients with secondary hyperparathyroidism. Summary of Risk Factors
agent1_snippets.content: Diagnosis relies on identifying a persistently low calcium level below 8.4 mg/dL (2.1 mmol/L) lasting more than 4 days postoperatively, accompanied by hypophosphatemia and normal PTH levels. Hypomagnesemia and hypocalciuria are frequently associated findings. [1] [2] [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Paget Bone Disease -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Tests to assist in the diagnosis of Paget disease include the following: Bone scan Bone x-ray Elevated markers of bone breakdown, such as N-telopeptide The disease may also present with the following findings: Elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP) Normal serum calcium and phosphate Measurement of serum ALP is useful, as are urine levels of hydroxyproline, C-telopeptide, and N-telopeptide. Procollagen N-terminal peptide is also a sensitive serum marker for bone formation. Hyperuricemia is common and is due to a high turnover of bone. Secondary hyperparathyroidism occurs in approximately 10% of patients due to inadequate calcium in the face of increased demand. [13] Plain x-rays may reveal cortical and trabecular thickening, osteosclerosis that looks like arthritis, bone expansion, or osteolytic areas. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Hungry Bone Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
agent1_snippets.content: When considering the possible etiologies for hypocalcemia, specifically in the postoperative period, it is essential to assess the condition of the parathyroid glands. Possibilities include postsurgical devascularization or accidental destruction and removal of the remaining parathyroid glands. In other cases, the remaining parathyroid glands may require additional time to recover from the prolonged suppression of their PTH production caused by the hyperactive adenoma. In these instances, unlike in HBS, hypocalcemia will be present, but PTH levels will be low, and phosphorus levels will be elevated. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Calcitonin -- Indications -- Paget disease of bone
agent1_snippets.content: As Paget's disease of bone is a chronic condition, calcitonin therapy can be sustained indefinitely. Patients are initially administered 50 to 100 units of calcitonin daily through IM or SQ administration, followed by a maintenance dose of either 50 units daily or 50 to 100 units every 1 to 3 days. Serum ALP should be measured at 3 to 6 months to assess patients' response to calcitonin therapy. When serum ALP levels have returned to normal, they can be evaluated every 6 months to a year. If ALP levels start to increase again, the possibility of calcitonin antibody formation should be considered; therefore, an antibody titer test may be warranted. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation are also recommended for patients, followed by periodic monitoring of their serum levels. If the patient continues to experience bone pain despite calcitonin therapy, obtaining a computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan can provide more precise insight into the lesion and aid in deciding whether surgical intervention should be considered.

agent1_snippets.title: Calcium -- Pathophysiology -- Calcium and the Musculoskeletal System
agent1_snippets.content: Bone tissue stores calcium as hydroxyapatite, deposited when serum calcium levels are elevated and released when levels are low. This process is linked with the endocrine system via the thyroid and parathyroid glands. When serum calcium levels are elevated, the thyroid gland releases calcitonin, which inhibits bone resorption by halting osteoclast activity. The PTH effect on bone tissue is dependent on continuous versus intermittent exposure. [14] In continuous exposure, PTH activates osteoclasts more readily by enhancing RANKL, which increases calcium resorption and bone loss, promoting an osteoporotic state. [17] Intermittent PTH exposure favors osteoblast activation, which promotes bone formation. However, chronically reduced PTH levels precipitate decreased bone remodeling and, subsequently, weak and brittle bones. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Osteomalacia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Definite osteomalacia is defined as having all of the 5 above findings. Possible osteomalacia is defined as having the first 2 findings and 2 of the 3 remaining findings described above. In another review by Uday and Hogler, new criteria were proposed for the diagnosis of osteomalacia, and include the following: Elevated PTH levels Elevated alkaline phosphatase levels Low urine calcium levels Low calcium intake (usually <300 mg/day) or low calcidiol levels (<30 nmol/L) [22] The use of the above criteria can help with the diagnosis of osteomalacia only in cases where kidney or liver problems are lacking.

agent1_snippets.title: Calcitonin -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Extensive research has been conducted on salmon calcitonin, revealing its heightened potency compared to the human variant, thus establishing it as the preferred option in clinical application. Currently, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved calcitonin for the management of postmenopausal osteoporosis, Paget's disease of bone, and hypercalcemia. The drug's clinical significance derives from its ability to inhibit osteoclast activity and increase the renal excretion of calcium. These mechanisms diminish the resorption of bone matrix and serum calcium levels.

agent1_snippets.title: Hypoparathyroidism -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: The absence of PTH reduces the renal tubular reabsorption of calcium. Therefore, patients treated for hypoparathyroidism are at risk of urolithiasis or renal and other soft tissue calcifications. These risks can be minimized by titrating therapy to keep the serum calcium level in the low-normal range and avoid hypercalciuria. Once approved for clinical use, selected patients who are more challenging to control on traditional therapy could benefit from daily subcutaneous injections of an analog of parathyroid hormone. Parathyroid hormone replacement can substantially reduce oral calcium and calcitriol requirements, restore more physiological bone turnover, and improve quality of life.

agent1_snippets.title: Hyperphosphatemic Tumoral Calcinosis -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: As there is a lack of good-quality clinical trials in HTC, given the rarity of the disorder, the treatment options are mainly based on previous observational studies, case series, and case reports. As such, the medical management for HTC continues to evolve as newly published evidence becomes available. The medical therapies chiefly focus on lowering serum phosphate levels and decreasing the levels of inflammatory markers. However, the ideal therapy for the heritable form of HTC would be gene-replacement therapy for the defective gene. Until the time becomes available, research should also focus on hormone replacement therapy with iFGF23 to regulate serum phosphate levels.

agent1_snippets.title: Calcitonin -- Indications -- Paget disease of bone
agent1_snippets.content: In a study involving 24 patients with untreated Paget disease of bone, calcitonin administration reduced skeletal blood flow to the affected Paget bone. This effect could lead to decreased disease activity and lessened bleeding during surgical interventions. The markers of bone remodeling and turnover, namely serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and urine hydroxyproline, were also reduced. [10] A separate study involving 85 patients similarly exhibited 50% reductions in ALP and urine hydroxyproline levels over 3 to 6 months. However, among these individuals, 22 reverted to baseline values despite continuous treatment. Interestingly, out of these 22 patients, 19 developed elevated titers of anti-calcitonin antibodies. [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Osteoporosis in Females -- Differential Diagnosis
agent1_snippets.content: When evaluating osteoporosis in females, it is essential to consider other conditions that can mimic or contribute to decreased bone density. The following is a list of differential diagnoses that should be ruled out during assessment. The differential diagnosis for osteoporosis is as follows: Osteopenia Osteogenesis imperfecta Osteomalacia Vitamin D deficiency or insufficiency Hyperparathyroidism Hyperthyroidism Paget disease of the bone Mineral and bone disorders of chronic kidney disease Primary bone malignancies Metastases to the skeleton Avascular necrosis Hematologic malignancies Mimickers of vertebral osteoporosis (such as osteoarthritis, scoliosis, or Scheuermann disease) [188] [189]

agent1_snippets.title: Osteomalacia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: No single laboratory finding is specific to osteomalacia. However, patients with osteomalacia typically have hypophosphatemia, hypocalcemia, or both. Additionally, increased alkaline phosphatase activity is typically characteristic of any disease with impaired osteoid mineralization. Some sources believe that hypophosphatemia or hypocalcemia and increased bone alkaline phosphatase levels are necessary to suspect osteomalacia. Low bone mineral density (BMD) and focal uptake at Looser zones can appear on plain x-rays and bone scintigraphy as the disease progresses. Fukumoto et al proposed the following findings of definite or possible osteomalacia, which require validation with further studies: Hypophosphatemia or hypocalcemia High bone alkaline phosphatase Muscle weakness or bone pain Less than 80% BMD of the young adult–mean Multiple uptake zones by bone scintigraphy or radiographic evidence of Looser zones (pseudofractures) [18] [21]

agent1_snippets.title: Osteomalacia -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: In processes that decrease the amount of vitamin D or its bioproducts, normal serum calcium will be maintained by mobilizing calcium from the bones. Specifically, PTH will be secreted by the parathyroid glands in response to hypocalcemia from vitamin D deficiency in an attempt to restore calcium homeostasis. Bones are the primary site of calcium recruitment, and osteomalacia will ensue by extracting calcium from the bones. Therefore, adults affected by processes that disrupt vitamin D metabolism and production are at risk for eventually developing osteomalacia and its clinical manifestations. [1] [2] [3]

agent1_snippets.content: Once identified and an appropriate treatment plan is in place, laboratory values may begin to normalize within weeks of initiation. Overall, complete healing of osteomalacia may vary and can take many months to over a year, depending on the initial cause.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
agent2_snippets.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
agent2_snippets.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional group “hormones by function.” It is employed therapeutically to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia, a deficiency of calcium in the blood). Parathormon has a physiological relationship with kalcitonin (calcitonin); the two hormones interact to regulate calcium homeostasis, often exerting opposing effects to maintain appropriate serum calcium levels. Understanding the role of parathormon in calcium deficiency treatment and its interplay with kalcitonin is essential for effective management of disorders of calcium metabolism.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1592
agent2_snippets.content: Parathormon is classified within the functional group “hormones by function” and its disposition is that of a hormone. As a hormone, parathormon produces a specific physiologic effect: it leads to decreased renal Ca++ excretion. This reduction in renal calcium loss is a key component of parathormon’s role in maintaining calcium homeostasis, and recognizing that parathormon belongs to the hormone category and exerts this effect is important for the clinical management of calcium‑related disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
agent2_snippets.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
agent2_snippets.content: Cinacalcet is classified as an anti‑parathyroid agent, meaning it belongs to the drug class that suppresses parathyroid hormone (PTH) secretion. By activating calcium‑sensing receptors on parathyroid cells, cinacalcet reduces PTH levels, which is particularly useful in managing secondary hyperparathyroidism associated with chronic kidney disease and other disorders of calcium‑phosphate metabolism. Recognizing cinacalcet as an anti‑parathyroid agent guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, monitoring calcium and phosphate balance, and optimizing patient outcomes in conditions where excess PTH is detrimental.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 736
agent2_snippets.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional category “hormones by function” and belongs to the group “parathyroid hormones and analogues.” It is therapeutically employed to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia). Calcitonin has a physiological relationship with parathormon, with the two hormones exerting opposing actions that regulate calcium balance in the body. Teriparatid is an analogue that is a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” class, mimicking the activity of parathormon. Together, these connections underscore the central role of parathormon and its analogues in maintaining calcium homeostasis and in the clinical management of hypocalcemia.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1755
agent2_snippets.content: Kalcitonin and parathormon are interrelated hormones that play complementary roles in calcium homeostasis. Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is a member of the broader class of parathyroid hormones and analogues. Kalcitonin, secreted by thyroid C‑cells, acts to lower serum calcium levels, counterbalancing the calcium‑raising effects of parathormon, which increases calcium by stimulating bone resorption, enhancing renal calcium reabsorption, and activating vitamin D. Recognizing the relationship between kalcitonin and parathormon, and that parathormon belongs to the group of parathyroid hormones and analogues, is essential for understanding and managing disorders of calcium metabolism.
